title: Listening - Part 3: Journalism and AI
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with two journalists, Alistair and Fiona, discussing the impact of artificial intelligence on their profession. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview with Alistair and Fiona regarding the ethical and professional challenges of AI in the newsroom.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Welcome. Today we’re discussing the seismic shifts in the media landscape. Joining me are Alistair, a veteran foreign correspondent, and Fiona, a digital editor for a national daily. Alistair, many are calling AI the 'death knell' of investigative reporting. Do you share that grim outlook?

Alistair: It’s easy to get swept up in the doomsday rhetoric. However, if we look back at the arrival of the internet, we heard the same prophecies of doom. [15]What I find frustrating is the assumption that a machine can replicate the sheer doggedness required for an investigation. An algorithm can process a leak of a million documents in seconds, which is undeniably useful, but it cannot sit in a dimly lit café and build trust with a whistleblower. It lacks the intuition to know when a source is holding back out of fear rather than deceit. [/15] So, while it’s a powerful tool for data crunching, the 'human' element of sniffing out a story remains, in my view, entirely safe from automation.

Interviewer: Fiona, as someone managing a digital newsroom, surely you see the efficiency gains as a positive?

Fiona: Efficiency is a double-edged sword. Yes, we use automated systems for routine financial reports or sports scores, which frees up my staff for more complex features. [16]But there’s a subtle, more insidious danger in the way AI prioritizes engagement. We’re seeing a trend where 'news' is being shaped by what the algorithm predicts will go viral. This creates a feedback loop where nuance is sacrificed for outrage, because that’s what the machine has learned generates clicks. [/16] My concern isn't that robots will write the news, but that they will dictate what qualifies as news in the first place, leading to a narrower, more polarized public discourse.

Interviewer: Alistair, you’ve recently written about the 'dilution' of the profession. Is that a result of these new tools?

Alistair: Partly. [17]What worries me is the blurring of the line between a curated piece of journalism and a generative summary. When readers can't distinguish between an article that took three months of boots-on-the-ground research and a two-second AI compilation of existing web content, the value of the 'brand' of journalism starts to evaporate. [/17] We are moving toward a world where 'information' is free and abundant, but 'truth' is becoming increasingly expensive and rare. If we don't find a way to verify and label human-generated content, we risk a total collapse of public trust.

Interviewer: Fiona, Alistair mentioned trust. How does a digital-first paper maintain that when deepfakes and AI-generated misinformation are everywhere?

Fiona: It’s an arms race, frankly. [18]We’ve had to invest heavily in verification tech, but more importantly, we’ve doubled down on transparency. We now provide 'provenance' links for our major scoops—showing the raw footage, the interview transcripts, and the data sets. It’s no longer enough to say 'trust us because we’re a famous newspaper.' We have to show our homework. [/18] Paradoxically, AI might actually force us to become better, more transparent journalists because the stakes for being wrong have never been higher.

Interviewer: Alistair, what about the younger generation entering the field? Should they be worried?

Alistair: [19]They should be adaptable. The era of the specialist who only writes about one niche is probably over. The next generation needs to be 'AI-literate'—not necessarily coders, but they must understand the biases of the tools they use. My fear is for the entry-level roles. Those junior reporting jobs where you’d learn the ropes by covering local council meetings are being automated. [/19] We’re losing the 'training ground' for future greats, and that’s a structural problem for the industry that I don't think we've solved yet.

Interviewer: Fiona, final thoughts on the future of the newsroom?

Fiona: [20]I believe we will see a return to 'slow journalism.' Just as the rise of fast food led to a movement for artisanal, slow-cooked meals, the deluge of AI-generated content will create a premium market for deep, slow, human-centric storytelling. [/20] People will eventually crave the perspective of someone who has actually 'been there,' and they will be willing to pay for that authenticity.

Interviewer: Alistair, Fiona, a fascinating discussion. Thank you.

It lacks the intuition to know when a source is holding back out of fear rather than deceit. So, while it’s a powerful tool for data crunching, the 'human' element of sniffing out a story remains, in my view, entirely safe from automation.

questions.question: Alistair believes that investigative journalism is secure because AI
options: A) cannot analyze large data leaks effectively.
B) lacks the personal intuition needed to handle sources.
C) is too expensive for most newsrooms to implement.
D) tends to produce inaccurate financial reports.
questions.explanation: Alistair argues that AI cannot build trust with whistleblowers or sense when a source is fearful, which are essential 'human' elements.

questions.question: What is Fiona’s main concern regarding AI and news engagement?
options: A) It will make digital editors redundant.
B) It encourages a focus on sensationalism over complexity.
C) It is unable to predict which stories will become popular.
D) It primarily promotes local stories over international news.
questions.explanation: Fiona notes that algorithms prioritize 'outrage' and 'clicks' over nuance, shaping the news based on viral potential.

questions.question: When discussing the 'dilution' of journalism, Alistair points out that
options: A) information is becoming too difficult for the public to access.
B) readers prefer AI summaries to long-form articles.
C) the distinction between research and compilation is disappearing.
D) big brands are no longer interested in investigative work.
questions.explanation: He worries that readers can't distinguish between 'boots-on-the-ground' research and AI compilations, devaluing the journalistic brand.

questions.question: Fiona suggests that the rise of deepfakes has led her newspaper to
options: A) stop publishing controversial scoops.
B) rely solely on historical data for their reporting.
C) use AI to generate their own verification software.
D) provide evidence of their reporting process to the public.
questions.explanation: She mentions providing 'provenance' links and 'showing their homework' to maintain trust through transparency.

questions.question: What is Alistair's fear for the future generation of journalists?
options: A) They will need to spend years learning how to code.
B) There are fewer opportunities to learn basic skills on the job.
C) They are becoming too specialized in narrow niches.
D) They are overly skeptical of helpful new technologies.
questions.explanation: Alistair laments the automation of 'junior reporting jobs' which served as the essential 'training ground' for the profession.

questions.question: Fiona compares the future of journalism to the 'slow food' movement to imply that
options: A) quality reporting will become a luxury product.
B) journalism will eventually become a non-profit industry.
C) the public will lose interest in international affairs.
D) fast-paced digital news will eventually disappear.
questions.explanation: She suggests that there will be a 'premium market' for authentic, human-centric storytelling as a reaction to AI-generated content.
